title: W1 Logistics
body:
ECON 3202 – Mathematical Economics


          Juan Carlos Carbajal

       School of Economics UNSW–Sydney



              Term 1 — 2026




                                         1/11
 Introduction




               2/11
                          Purpose of the Course




A course on mathematical economics should:
  • provide a stepping stone to Honours programs and other advanced
    undergraduate courses;

  • provide a rich foundation to complex problem solving in economics;

  • formalise the intuition behind important economic insights.


               A competitive equilibrium is Pareto efficient.

The optimal labour contract under moral hazard solves the trade-off between
               insurance and incentives for effort provision.




                                                                              3/11
                            Focus of ECON3202




We have been continually redeveloping mathematical economics since
2020 in response to feedback from Honours students:
  • “Less time on simple differentiation techniques.”

  • “More time on abstract mathematics and formal proofs.”

  • “More economic examples to illustrate the mathematical concepts.”


We now focus on understanding deep mathematical concepts that are
essential for economics.




                                                                        4/11
                              Course Outline


The course is divided in two parts: foundations and optimisation.

Foundations
  • Logic and set theory.

  • Operations in Euclidean vector spaces.

  • Elements of topology

  • Real-valued functions and their properties.

  • Sequences, limits and continuity.

  • Linear functions.




                                                                    5/11
                              Course Outline




The course is divided in two parts: foundations and optimisation.

Optimisation
  • Multivariate calculus.

  • Concave and convex functions.

  • Unconstrained optimisation.

  • Constrained optimisation.




                                                                    6/11
 Logistics

                                                                           8/11
                                References


• The website for this course is on UNSW Moodle at
  http://moodle.telt.unsw.edu.au

• The required textbook for this course is
    – Jehle, Geoffrey A., Philip J. Reny, Advanced Microeconomic Theory,
      Pearson, 2011. Mathematical Appendix.


• Additional sources include
    – Sundaram, Rangarajan K., A First Course in Optimization Theory,
      Cambridge University Press, 1996.

    – Chiang, A., K. Wainwright, Fundamental Methods of Mathematical
      Economics, McGraw-Hill, 2005.




                                                                           9/11
                                 Assessments

Problem Sets (48 marks)
 • Each problem set will consist of between two and five problems.

 • You must submit your solutions as a PDF document via Moodle before
   14:00 on the due date.
 • Due dates for submission:
     – PS 01 due Wed March 4;

     – PS 02 due Wed March 18;

     – PS 03 due Wed April 8;

     – PS 04 due Wed April 22;

Final Exam (52 marks)
 • The in-person final exam will consist of between two and five problems.
 • It will be held during Exam Period.



                                                                             10/11
                          Lectures and Tutorials



Lectures
  • We will meet beginning Tuesday week 1.

  • Some of the relevant material will be available via pre-recorded videos
    posted on Moodle.


Tutorials
  • G V A will run tutorials beginning week 2.

  • Tutorial for week 8 will be available as a pre-recorded video due to
    public holidays.
